questions.prompt: Choose the correct main clause: "If you press this button, the machine ___,"
questions.explanation: Pressing the button always makes the machine start, so this is a zero conditional (a reliable fact): both clauses use the present simple, hence "the machine starts". "started" is past simple; "would start" is second conditional (imaginary); "will starting" is not a valid verb form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "If I ___ you, I would talk to the teacher about this problem."
questions.explanation: "If I were you" gives advice about an imaginary situation, so it is a second conditional. In standard English, "be" takes "were" for every person in the if-clause: If I were you. "am" is the real present; "was" is the common informal form but not the exam-standard one; "would be" belongs in the main clause, not the if-clause.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is a correct first conditional?

options.text: If it will rain, we stay inside.
If it rained, we will stay inside.
If it rains, we would stay inside.
If it rains, we will stay inside.

questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present simple (rains) + will + base verb (will stay), as in option d. Option a wrongly puts "will" in the if-clause; option b mixes past simple ("rained") with "will"; option c uses "would" in the main clause, which belongs to the second conditional.

questions.prompt: Complete: "___ you finish early, you can leave." (use unless or if)
questions.explanation: The sentence means: finishing early is the condition for being allowed to leave. This is a positive condition, so use "if": if you finish early, you can leave. "Unless" would mean the opposite.

questions.prompt: Choose the sentence where the comma is used correctly:

options.text: We will cancel the trip, if it snows.
If it snows we will cancel the trip.
We will cancel, the trip if it snows.
If it snows, we will cancel the trip.

questions.explanation: The comma is placed after the if-clause when it comes first in the sentence: "If it snows, we will cancel the trip." No comma is needed when the main clause comes first (option a has an unnecessary comma).

questions.prompt: Complete both blanks: "If she ___ the exam, her parents ___ very proud."
questions.explanation: First conditional: if + present simple (passes) + will + base verb (will be). The situation is realistic — she may well pass. Option c mixes past simple with will (second conditional main clause with first conditional if-clause), which is incorrect.
